question_text: 1. The crescent represents the Muslims of India, while the seven stars are the 'saptrishi constellation' and represent the myriad Hindu communities. The Union Jack was to denote greater autonomy and _________ as a dominion of the British Empire. FITB and name the two persons who proposed this flag. [image: historical Indian flag with crescent, seven stars, and Union Jack]
answer_text: Home Rule; Annie Besant and Lokmanya Tilak

discussion.text: Trying to think of the second person

question_text: 2. This flag of _______ uses the standard red ensign design and features the erstwhile seal of this province. The seal features two ships and a structure named after King George III, that was originally built to guard the newly acquired territory against possible enemy incursions, including from the French. This structure was demolished in the mid-late 19th century, but it lends its name to an iconic part of ________ (same blank) [image: British Indian province flag with red ensign design and provincial seal]
answer_text: Bombay Presidency; the neighborhood is Fort (named after Fort George)

discussion.text: Is the neighborhood the newly acquired territory they were trying to guard?
Yes this structure was built in Bombay.
It was built at the site of an older structure called the Dongri _______ from earlier times
Dockyard? I am just making stuff up now
Fort George was the garrison the British built to protect Bombay from invasions

question_text: 4. This historical flag features two cobras flanking the sun, denoting both the founders' claimed lineage from the Nagas and suryavanshis, and also symbolizing a likely apocryphal story about Mahadji _________ being shielded from the sun by cobras as a child. The saffron background is a common feature of successor states descended from the ______ confederacy. Which state and dynasty? This royal house still exists and this pic is from their ancestral palace in _________. [image: saffron flag with two cobras flanking the sun, ancestral palace of the Scindia dynasty]
answer_text: Scindia (Shinde) dynasty of Gwalior; descended from the Maratha confederacy
confirmation_text: Correct! The Scindias (or Shindes) of Gwalior is correct!

discussion.text: I know the Gaekwad dynasty has a saffron flag but doesn't have cobras. Am I in the right area?
no actually Scindia and Gwalior i think
So yes, the saffron background is common to almost all states coming out of the Marathas
Correct! The Scindias (or Shindes) of Gwalior is correct!

question_text: Five ships — Concepción, San Antonio, Santiago, Trinidad and Victoria — set sail from Seville with about 250 people under the command of a Portuguese sailor. Just one of them — Victoria — made it to the destination with 18 survivors, after the original leader of the expedition died on the way. What remarkable feat did this expedition accomplish?
answer_text: First circumnavigation of the globe; expedition was led by Ferdinand Magellan (Portuguese, sailing for Spain)
confirmation_text: Correct, Magellan was also Portuguese, just 17 of the 250 made it back to Europe after circumnavigating the globe

discussion.text: Is this Vasco da Gama arriving in India?
Correct, Magellan was also Portuguese, just 17 of the 250 made it back to Europe after circumnavigating the globe

question_text: One of the places this expedition stopped was the kingdom of Butuan. They observed Hindu religious practices there. It is the place farthest from India where Hindu religious practices were observed before Europeans colonized the world. Which country is the kingdom of Butuan in?
answer_text: Philippines
confirmation_text: The Philippines is the correct answer, they found lots of evidence of trade with Srivijaya (Indonesia), Champa (Vietnam), Cambodia, etc. in Butuan

discussion.text: The Philippines is the correct answer, they found lots of evidence of trade with Srivijaya (Indonesia), Champa (Vietnam), Cambodia, etc. in Butuan

question_text: 5. This flag of a British Indian province features interesting symbols, including a bow, two matsyas depicting religious diversity, and a Y-symbol depicting something evocative. Which province? This seal lives on as the official seal of the present-day state. [image: British Indian province flag with bow, two fish, and Y-shaped river symbol]
answer_text: United Provinces (present-day Uttar Pradesh); the Y-symbol depicts the Triveni Sangam at Prayagraj, the fish depict Awadh, and the bow depicts the Ramayana

confluence of Ganga and Yamuna, religious diversity
Denotes the sangam at prayagraj. The fish denote Awadh

question_text: 6. This kingdom and later princely state is believed to have been founded by the brother of the last Kakatiya king of Warangal. The trishul and moon denote Lord Shiva, possibly referencing the temple dedicated to Goddess Sati located in its largest town, and today the HQ of the district of the same name. [image: flag of the Bastar princely state with trishul and moon symbols]
answer_text: Bastar (princely state, now Bastar district in Chhattisgarh); the town is Dantewada, where Sati's tooth (dant) is said to have fallen

discussion.text: Nope, the goddess sati is a hint, expanding further - the temple and the town derives its name from the legend that one of Sati's teeth fell over there.
Bastar is correct, today Bastar district in CG. Their descendants have also been active in politics on both sides of the spectrum

question_text: 7. Probably the easiest in this set. ID this princely state, arguably the most powerful pre-independence, very close to the British Raj and a formidable opponent of the Republic of India. [image: flag of Hyderabad state with Nizam's symbol]
answer_text: Hyderabad (Nizam's state)

question_text: 8. Flag showing what and representing what entity? Today it's known as _______. By way of hint, this symbol has a lot of significance in Indian culture and Hinduism. [image: saffron flag with a lotus symbol, flag of the Bharatiya Jana Sangh (BJS)]
answer_text: Bharatiya Jana Sangh (BJS) flag; today known as the BJP (Bharatiya Janata Party)

question_text: X and Y were a director-actor duo infamous for their volatile collaborations on five films between 1972 and 1987, including one where Y allegedly threatened X with a gun during production and another involving dragging a steamship over a mountain in the Amazon. X, a prolific filmmaker who once ate his own shoe after losing a bet, is known for his intense documentaries, while Y, a temperamental star with over 130 film credits, reportedly attempted to direct traffic in Rome while naked. Who are X and Y?
answer_text: X = Werner Herzog (director), Y = Klaus Kinski (actor)

discussion.text: Just going by documentaries is it herzog?
Yep, Herzog is X. The actor is German, a frequent collaborator, and one on whom he also made a documentary titled My dear fiend.
Yup, KK is the non-cryptic hint

question_text: This popular children's toy/product was first marketed as a soot remover from walls of the houses. As gas and oil heaters became popular in the 1940s, it fell out of favor and sales started dipping. It got reinvented as the product we know today for art and craft, and is popular globally.
answer_text: Play-Doh

question_text: Talking about United Provinces… this was the name of Uttar Pradesh under British rule. But before it became the name of that region, it was the name of an entirely different country. Which country used to be called United Provinces in the 17th and 18th centuries?
answer_text: The Netherlands (United Provinces of the Netherlands)
confirmation_text: Correct! During the American revolution, the United Provinces of the Netherlands helped out with loans to the Americans

discussion.text: That dutch area? I don't know if it's more countries than Netherlands
Correct! During the American revolution, the United Provinces of the Netherlands helped out with loans to the Americans

question_text: The name UP of India itself is a shortened version, formalized in 1937, from United Provinces of _____ and _____?
answer_text: United Provinces of Agra and Oudh

discussion.text: Not sure what the West side was called
Was Meerut a province? Or Agra maybe
UP of Agra and Oudh (their spelling for awadh)

question_text: The capital of British India was ____ until 1911. The capital was shifted to Delhi that year. Before that, Delhi and its surroundings were part of a different province of British India. What was the previous capital, and which province was Delhi part of before it became the capital?
answer_text: Capital was Calcutta (Kolkata); Delhi was part of the Punjab province

discussion.text: Which province was Delhi part of before it became the capital?

question_text: R.K. Narayan wrote his best-known work _The Guide_ in 1956 during a visit to the United States. It was his first trip outside India, funded by a Rockefeller Fellowship. All the friends I seem to have in the world seem to be gathered there." Name the town.

question_text: This is a photo of a politician in chains taken during India's Emergency in 1976. This photo was published in international media including _Time_ magazine and became a symbol of a country in chains. This politician went on to serve as the union minister for industry, railways, and defense under different prime ministers. He spoke Konkani, Tulu, Kannada, Tamil, Malayalam, Hindi, Marathi, and English, and was elected five times to the Lok Sabha from the Muzaffarpur constituency in Bihar. Name him. [image: politician in chains, Emergency era India 1976]
answer_text: George Fernandes

discussion.text: The 1976 arrest was the dynamite conspiracy IIRC. Some plan about blowing up a train or looting a train carrying dynamite.

question_text: He is considered the most important Buddhist philosopher after the Buddha himself. He founded the _mAdhyamaka_ ("middle way") school of _mahAyAna_ ("great vehicle") Buddhism. His influence on Buddhist philosophy is greater than that of Adi Shankara on Hindu philosophy. Little is known reliably about his personal life, as the only extant biographies are in Chinese and Tibetan, and were written centuries after his death. Some sources say he served as the head of the university at nAlanda. Independent India named a university and a dam after him. Who is he?
answer_text: Nagarjuna (Buddhist philosopher; Nagarjuna Sagar dam and Nagarjuna University named after him)

Most of the chinese and japanese icons are Indian
I am going to say bodhidharma?
Nagarjuna was a buddhist saint I dunno if it is this though
Nagarjuna should be it I think.
Nagarjuna is the most influential Buddhist philosopher
Nagarjuna Sagar and Nagarjuna University are named after him

question_text: This is a funny play on words of what phrase originally said by Leonidas to Xerxes at the Battle of Thermopylae, and also referenced in the movie 300. It symbolizes freedom, defiance and resistance against tyranny. Bonus pts for the original Greek phrase. [image: sign or item reading 'Come and mow it' — a play on 'Come and take it' / 'Molon Labe']
answer_text: Come and take it (original Greek: Molon Labe — said by Leonidas at Thermopylae, used by 2A advocates in the US)
confirmation_text: Come and take it is correct

discussion.text: Come and take it is correct
Leonidas is believed to have said 'molon labe' which is 'come and take them' in the context of surrendering

question_text: Not much remarkable about this flag, except that it has the typical red-yellow color combination common to most Hindu kingdoms due to its auspiciousness in Hinduism, and the sun symbol denotes the claimed suryavanshi lineage, again common for kshatriya kingdoms. [image: red and yellow flag with sun symbol, flag of the Cooch Behar princely state]
answer_text: Cooch Behar (princely state in West Bengal; Maharani Gayatri Devi was also a princess of Cooch Behar)
